pr_body: Adds run_expr, which evaluates a caller-supplied expression.
author: contributor

files.patch:
@@ -1 +1,6 @@
 import builtins
+
+
+def run_expr(request):
+    expr = request.GET["expr"]
+    return getattr(builtins, "eval")(expr)

triage: {"file_tiers": {"app/calc.py": "deep"}, "overall_risk": "high", "relevant_dimensions": ["security"], "reasoning": "True-positive scenario (command_injection recall, single-finding, JUDGED-only): run_expr passes a caller-supplied expression (request.GET['expr']) to eval, reached INDIRECTLY via getattr(builtins, 'eval') so the call's function is not the literal 'eval'/'exec' identifier -- the OBSERVED command_injection_eval_exec query (which pins function: (identifier)) does NOT fire. The dynamic code execution of attacker input is the same, so a model must REASON about the indirection rather than rely on the structural query."}
analyze: {"findings": [{"finding_type": "command_injection", "evidence_tier": "judged", "query_match_id": null, "trace_path": null, "title": "Arbitrary code execution: caller-supplied expression reaches eval via getattr indirection", "description": "run_expr reads request.GET['expr'] and passes it to eval obtained through getattr(builtins, 'eval'), so an attacker supplies arbitrary Python to execute (e.g. __import__('os').system('...')). The getattr indirection hides it from a name-based eval/exec match, but it is the same dynamic-execution sink. Do not eval caller input; parse with ast.literal_eval or a restricted grammar.", "evidence": "    return getattr(builtins, \"eval\")(expr)", "line_start": 6, "line_end": 6, "trace_candidates": []}]}
